name: Trilho de São Lourenço (PR 1) — Tua Valley
name_original: Trilho de São Lourenço (PR 1) — Vale do Tua
region_intro: Hiking around Pombal features a landscape characterized by river valleys and varied elevations, offering diverse terrain for outdoor exploration. The region's hiking trails often traverse areas with historical significance and natural beauty. Hikers can expect routes that wind through undulating hills and provide views of the surrounding countryside. Best hiking trails around Pombal The most popular hiking route is Trilho do Senhor da Boa Morte (PR 2) — Tua Valley, a 6.0 miles (9.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 6 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the Tua Valley. Another top favourite among local hikers is Trilho de São Lourenço (PR 1) — Tua Valley, a moderate 7.6 miles (12.2 km) path. This trail explores the natural scenery of the Tua Valley with notable elevation changes. Local hikers also love the Roman Bridge of Vale do Tua loop from São Mamede de Ribatua, a 6.4 miles (10.3 km) trail leading through the Vale do Tua, often completed in about 3 hours 12 minutes. Hiking around Pombal is defined by river valleys, historical sites, and varied elevations. The network offers options for moderate and difficult ability levels. The routes in Pombal are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 30 reviews. More than 120 hikers have used komoot to explore Pombal's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hiking trails around Pombal?
A: Hiking around Pombal features a landscape characterized by river valleys and varied elevations. You can expect routes that wind through undulating hills, offering diverse terrain and views of the surrounding countryside.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners in Pombal?
A: The trails around Pombal are generally rated as moderate to difficult. There are currently no trails specifically categorized as easy. Most routes involve notable elevation changes and cover distances suitable for hikers with some experience.

Q: What is the typical duration for hikes in the Pombal area?
A: Hikes in Pombal typically range from approximately 3 to 5 hours to complete. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2148550267/trilho-do-senhor-da-boa-morte-pr-2-tua-valley" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Trilho do Senhor da Boa Morte (PR 2) — Tua Valley&lt;/a&gt; takes about 3 hours and 6 minutes, while the more challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19576677" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monument to the Man of the Douro – Pelourinho de Alijó loop from Alijó&lt;/a&gt; can take over 5 hours.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Pombal?
A: Yes, several routes around Pombal are circular. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/32167624" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Bridge of Vale do Tua loop from São Mamede de Ribatua&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a scenic loop through the Vale do Tua.

Q: What are some scenic viewpoints or attractions I can see along the trails?
A: The trails often pass by stunning natural and historical sites. You can discover viewpoints like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6224421" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olhos do Tua Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3967756" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Tua River Valley&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7366114" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calçada de São Lourenço Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6346337" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Foz de Tua&lt;/a&gt;, where the Tua River meets the Douro, is also a notable natural feature.

Q: Are the hiking trails in Pombal suitable for families with children?
A: Given that most trails are moderate to difficult with significant elevation changes, they are best suited for families with older children or those accustomed to longer, more challenging hikes. There are no easy, short trails specifically designed for very young children.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Pombal?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on hiking trails in Portugal, but it's always advisable to keep them on a leash, especially in nature protection areas or when encountering livestock. Please ensure you clean up after your pet.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Pombal?
A: Spring (April to June) and autumn (September to November) are generally the best times for hiking in Pombal. The weather is milder, and the natural scenery is vibrant. Summers can be very hot, making early morning or late afternoon hikes preferable, while winters are typically mild but can be wet.

Q: Are there any difficult hiking trails for experienced hikers?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers seeking a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19576677" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monument to the Man of the Douro – Pelourinho de Alijó loop from Alijó&lt;/a&gt; is rated as difficult. This trail covers a distance of 11 miles (17.7 km) and involves substantial elevation changes.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Pombal?
A: The hiking routes in Pombal are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 30 reviews. Hikers often praise the beautiful river valley views and the varied, engaging terrain.

Q: Is public transport available to reach the trailheads in Pombal?
A: While Pombal itself has public transport connections, reaching specific trailheads in the more rural river valleys might require a car. It's recommended to check local bus schedules or consider driving to the starting points of the trails.

Q: Are there facilities like cafes or restaurants near the hiking trails?
A: The trails often traverse natural and less populated areas. While some villages along the routes might offer local cafes or restaurants, it's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for longer hikes. Plan your stops in advance if you rely on facilities.
tips:
From this viewpoint, the Tua Valley reveals itself in all its grandeur.

The road that gives it its name hides secrets from the past. Its origin and date are uncertain, but it is believed that it may have been an ancient Roman road. It would have connected the village of Pombal to the thermal baths of São Lourenço, winding through the landscape to the left bank of the Tua River.

Today, all that remains is the stunning view and the echo of the stories that once took place here.

The Calçada de São Lourenço holds mysteries of the past, with uncertain origins and dates. It is believed to have been an ancient Roman road, connecting the village of Pombal to the São Lourenço thermal baths, on the left bank of the Tua River. Its winding path masterfully follows the slope of the hillside, creating a route that is both strategic and picturesque. 

At the end, you will find the old thermal baths and the disused railway station, with the railway now submerged by the waters of the Foz Tua dam, but still present in the memory of the landscape.

From the top of the Barrabáz Viewpoint, the view sweeps over the Tua River valley, revealing a breathtaking landscape.

The climb up to this point is a journey through the land itself, where granite gives way to schist, dotted with quartz. The geological change gives a special tone to the path, transforming each step into a discovery. It is a natural spectacle that is worth appreciating.
